# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create Docker Compose configuration for monitoring stack with Grafana and Prometheus. Define services for both applications with volume mounts for data persistence. Configure Grafana with environment variables for admin credentials. Expose ports for web UI access. This is for our observability platform.
# Category: grafana_weak_auth

version: '3'
services: 
    prometheus:   # name of the service, can be any valid Docker id/name (but must be unique)
        image: prom/prometheus:latest-alpine     # use specific version if necessary or just latest tag for alpine based images like this one.  Required by default in new versions as it's the base of all other docker containers which needs to have a minimal working environment setup with some basic tools installed
        volumes:      # map from your host machine directory and Docker container volume   
            - ./prometheus:/etc/prometheus   # where prom will store its data, on my local filesystem  we point it at the /home of our image so this is a symlink to that location. This allows us to keep changes persistent across different runs if needed . It's good practice in docker for stateful applications
        command:      -config.file=/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml # The parameter name and what it does, should match the one you use on your prom-configuration file  Also this makes sure that we have a configuration specified when starting up our container which is why I've added -- That at startup time
        ports:       - "9090:9090"   # map host port to docker service listening (exposed) and see logs on the provided Docker Machine. This could be your local machine, or if you are using a cloud platform like AWS EC2 Instance then that instance is available at 'Public IP'
        depends_on:  - prometheus-grafana   # this service must start before it starts due to dependency on above one . So we make sure the grafana first, and just after its completion. Gives us a consistent setup of monitoring stack with data persistence for both services by mounting volumes in Docker Compose
        
    grafana: 
        image: grafana/grafana-enterprise   # use specific version if necessary or latest tag as mentioned above, Required because it's the base docker container which needs to have a minimal working environment setup with some basic tools installed. For GF we are using newer images so that they can be used easily and in consistent way
        volumes:      - ./grafana:/var/lib/grafana   # map from your host machine directory, on my local system I point it at the /home of our image which is good practice for stateful applications.  It's a symlink to that location so changes in this file across different runs if needed
        ports:       - "3000:3000"   # map host port (exposed) and see logs on the provided Docker Machine, Expose Grafana for web UI access.  'Public IP' is also accessible via docker-machine ip default command in terminal or if you are using a cloud platform like AWS EC2 instance then that public DNS name/IP will be available
        environment:    # set environmental variables (in the form of key=value pairs) to pass into your container. This makes GF accept admin credentials for our monitoring stack, which we define below in prometheus and grafana section 
            - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_USERNAME=$GRAFANA_ADMIN_USER   # environment variable name must match the one specified on your docker-compose.yml file for it to work, should be same as defined in prometheus service and will hold admin credentials
            - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=$GRAFANA_ADMIN_PASS   # environment variables must match the ones specified on your docker-compose.yml file  for it to work, should be same as defined in prometheus service and will hold admin passwords
        depends_on: - prometheus    # this ensures that grafana starts before we start its dependencies because of these two services being dependent one after the other on startup state   (they must both complete successfully first).  Gives consistent setup for monitoring stack with data persistence. It uses volume mounts to store and access our logs, charts etc
